summary: High‑quality soundboard capture of a stellar June‑15‑1985 show with crisp China Cat > Rider, smoking Wheel → Gimme Some Lovin', and an inspired first set.
review: The Walker‑Scotton SBD delivers clear, well‑balanced audio (with only minor pitch quirks) that lets the band’s tight 1985 performance shine. Highlights include a flawless China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider, a soaring Wheel into Gimme Some Lovin', and a fiery Lost Sailor, while a few drum slippages on Big River and U.S. Blues keep the set from being perfect.

ai_rating.rationale: Consistently high praise for both musicianship and soundboard quality across nine reviews; minor criticisms are limited to a few song‑specific issues.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Generally clear, crisp mix with excellent instrument separation. Reviewers note slight pitch/speed anomalies (-1% on some sections) and a few missing encore tracks, but overall the SBD is preferred over other sources.

standout_songs: China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider
show_quality.setlist_flow: The first set builds nicely with strong openers and a fiery finish, while the second set delivers long, inventive jams and seamless segues (notably Wheel → GSL). Minor drumming hiccups prevent an outright flawless rating.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Garcia’s lead work is fast yet focused; he belts out lyrics on China>Rider and shows strong tone throughout, though he stumbles a bit on She Belongs.
band_member_comments.Phil: Lesh’s bass is powerful – “busts your speakers” on Northbound Train – and adds depth to the Fat sound on many jams.
band_member_comments.Keys: Brent’s unique intro on China Cat and his added layers on Lost Sailor and Might As Well are highlighted as standout keyboard moments.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill/Mickey provide a loose, energetic feel but exhibit occasional tightness issues (Big River) and out‑of‑sync playing on U.S. Blues.
